dating.basis: Excavated at Mohenjo-daro (DK area, 1928–29 season); dated by stratigraphic context within the Mature Harappan sequence.

summary: A steatite seal showing a horned, seated figure — possibly three-faced — surrounded by animals, with what many read as a yogic posture (mūlabandhāsana). John Marshall (1931) dubbed it 'proto-Śiva as Paśupati, lord of beasts.' Whether it depicts a deity ancestral to Śiva, a generic horned lord of animals (a motif with Near-Eastern parallels), or something unreadable without the script, is one of the field's longest-running open questions.
whyItMatters: This seal is the battleground object for the Indus–Hindu continuity debate — the strongest single visual for continuity, and the standard exhibit in critiques of over-reading. The atlas presents the readings side by side; the ledger handles the propaganda uses of Indus imagery (in both directions).
honestyNote: The Indus script is undeciphered. Every religious interpretation of Indus imagery — including this entry's title — is inference. Marshall's 'proto-Śiva' is a hypothesis with modern supporters (e.g. Parpola's Dravidian-Śaiva reading) and serious critics (Srinivasan, Sullivan). Continuity of specific motifs (zebu, pipal, water ritual) into later Indian religion is plausible and argued by scholars; proof of identity of belief is not available.

locations.name: Current holder: National Museum, New Delhi, India (seal 420); other Indus seals held at Lahore & Karachi museums and internationally
locations.note: custody split predates Partition-era distribution agreements

sources.label: Marshall, J., Mohenjo-daro and the Indus Civilization, Vol. I (1931), ch. 5 — the original 'proto-Śiva' reading
Srinivasan, D., "Unhinging Śiva from the Indus Civilization", Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society (1984) — the standard critique
Parpola, A., The Roots of Hinduism: The Early Aryans and the Indus Civilization (OUP, 2015) — continuity case
Possehl, G., The Indus Civilization (2002), ch. on religion

image.credit: unknown Indus Valley Civilization sealmaker from Mohenjodaro archaeological site
